attributes.stem:
The junior doctor on call is asked to review a 42-year-old woman admitted under the general surgical team following a thyroidectomy. She has developed tingling around her mouth and in her fingers and toes that began two hours ago.

On examination, she appears anxious. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her surgical wound appears clean and dry.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 125 g/L (115-165)

  • White cell count: 10.8 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelets: 250 × 10⁹/L (150-400)

  • Sodium: 135 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium: 3.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Chloride: 102 mmol/L (95-105)

  • Bicarbonate: 24 mmol/L (22-32)

  • Urea: 5.5 mmol/L (2.5-7.5)

  • Creatinine: 75 µmol/L (45-90)

  • Corrected calcium: 1.6 mmol/L (2.1-2.6)

  • Magnesium: 0.68 mmol/L (0.70-1.10)

  • Phosphate: 1.2 mmol/L (0.8-1.5)

  • Albumin: 38 g/L (35-50)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs of acute severe hypocalcaemia following recent thyroid surgery. Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia occurs in up to 30% of patients due to inadvertent removal or temporary stunning of the parathyroid glands. Severe hypocalcaemia is defined as serum calcium less than 1.9 mmol/L and/or the presence of symptoms at any level below the reference range. This requires urgent treatment to prevent complications such as tetany, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmias. The presence of perioral and peripheral paraesthesia, positive Chvostek's sign, and a corrected calcium of 1.6 mmol/L indicates clinically significant hypocalcaemia requiring urgent treatment.

Intravenous calcium gluconate is the most appropriate immediate treatment for severe symptomatic hypocalcaemia. It provides rapid correction of serum calcium and relief of symptoms. The usual dose is 10-20 mL of 10% calcium gluconate given slowly over 10-20 minutes.

Oral calcium carbonate would be insufficient for acute management of severe symptomatic hypocalcaemia. While oral supplementation will be part of the long-term management plan, the immediate situation requires parenteral calcium.

Oral calcitriol (activated vitamin D) is often used in conjunction with calcium supplementation for long-term management of post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia, but it would not provide the rapid correction needed in this acute situation.

Intravenous potassium chloride does not address the key clinical issue of hypocalcaemia. While her potassium is mildly low, it is not the cause of her current symptoms and can be managed with oral supplementation after the more acute issue has been treated.

Intravenous magnesium sulphate is not the priority for this patient. While magnesium deficiency can contribute to hypocalcaemia, this is not the primary issue in this case and can be addressed after calcium replacement is given.

After the acute management, this patient will likely require ongoing oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation until parathyroid function recovers, which typically occurs within six to 12 months in most cases. Some patients may require permanent supplementation if permanent hypoparathyroidism develops.
learningPoints: Severe symptomatic hypocalcaemia requires immediate intravenous calcium replacement.
Symptoms of hypocalcaemia include perioral and peripheral paraesthesia, muscle cramps, and tetany.
Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia is a common complication due to inadvertent removal or stunning of the parathyroid glands.
references: Schafer AL, Shoback DM. Hypocalcemia: Diagnosis and Treatment. [Updated 2016 Jan 3]. In: Feingold KR, Anawalt B, Blackman MR, et al., editors. Endotext [Internet]. South Dartmouth (MA): MDText.com, Inc.; 2000-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279022/

Goyal A, Anastasopoulou C, Ngu M, et al. Hypocalcemia. [Updated 2023 Oct 15]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430912/

Turner J, Gittoes N, Selby P. Society for Endocrinology endocrine emergency guidance: emergency management of acute hypocalcaemia in adult patients. Endocr Connect. 2016;5(5):G7–G8. doi: 10.1530/EC-16-0054. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1530/EC-16-0054
